1.synthesis: When any of them has a grievance against another, does he dare go to law before the unrighteous rather than the saints? (1 Corinthians 6:1.) Do they not know that the saints will judge the world? And if the world is to be judged by them, are they incompetent to try trivial cases? Do they not know that they are to judge angels? How much more then matters pertaining to this life! (1 Corinthians 6:2–3.) If they have cases, then let them appoint as judges those who have no standing in the church (1 Corinthians 6:4). He says this to shame them—is there not a wise person among them who can settle a dispute between brothers? (1 Corinthians 6:5.) But brother goes to law against brother, and before unbelievers (1 Corinthians 6:6). To have lawsuits at all against one another is already a defeat—why not suffer wrong? Why not be defrauded? But they wrong and defraud their own brothers (1 Corinthians 6:7–8). The unrighteous will not inherit God's kingdom; neither the sexually immoral, nor idolaters, nor adulterers, nor men who practice homosexuality, nor thieves, nor the greedy, nor drunkards, nor revilers, nor swindlers—such were some of them, but they were washed, sanctified, and justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and the Spirit of their God (1 Corinthians 6:9–11).
1.historical_context: The public law courts of Roman Corinth were a significant social institution; taking disputes to court was a culturally normal way to pursue grievances. But the courts were also deeply embedded in patronage networks—outcomes often depended on social connections and rhetorical skill rather than simple justice. Paul's argument against using these courts has both theological grounds (saints will judge the world and angels at the end; surely they can resolve present disputes internally) and ecclesiological ones (displaying community conflict before unbelievers damages the witness). The 'vice list' of verses 9–10 (which includes sexual sins, economic sins, and social vices) functions as a catalog of pre-conversion behavior that has been definitively left behind through the 'washed, sanctified, justified' triad of verse 11—one of Paul's most compressed summaries of conversion's transformation.
1.literary_structure: The irony is sharp: the community that will judge the world cannot judge a trivial internal dispute. The 'Do they not know?' repetition (vv.2, 3, 9, 15, 16, 19) is 1 Corinthians' characteristic rhetorical marker for reminding the community of what their conversion-instruction taught. The vice list and conversion-triad (vv.9–11) bracket the lawsuits discussion with an eschatological and sacramental frame: those who will inherit the kingdom (and therefore judge the world) cannot act like those who will not. The 'such were some of you' is a past-tense departure that asserts the decisive nature of the conversion transformation, grounds the ethical argument in identity rather than rule-following.
1.theological_application: The lawsuit passage teaches that the church's eschatological status (judging the world and angels at the last day) should shape its present social practices. The community that will participate in final judgment should be capable of resolving internal disputes without recourse to outside adjudication. The deeper ethical principle—'why not suffer wrong? Why not be defrauded?' (v.7)—is the cross-logic applied to economic conflict: the willingness to absorb loss rather than pursue litigation models the self-giving of Christ. But Paul's point is not merely to forbid lawsuits; it is to expose the underlying attitude: the brother who is willing to defraud a fellow believer and then drag him to court is living in a way inconsistent with the identity bestowed in baptism. The conversion triad (washed, sanctified, justified, v.11) grounds the ethical demand in what they have already become—the imperative follows from the indicative: you were washed into this new existence; now live from it.
1.christology: 'Washed, sanctified, and justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and by the Spirit of our God' (v.11) is a compressed trinitarian statement of salvation: the Lord Jesus Christ is the name invoked in baptism, the Spirit is the agent of the transformation, and God is the divine ground of the whole action. The future judicial role of believers ('judging angels,' v.3) is derivative of Christ's own eschatological lordship—they will judge because they belong to and participate in the one who is the final Judge. The vice list's condemned behaviors are those that characterize existence outside Christ's lordship; the conversion triad names what happens when one comes under it. The 'in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ' is not magical formula but the actual transfer of allegiance and identity that makes believers members of a new community with new possibilities of life.

12.synthesis: 'All things are lawful for me'—but not all things are helpful. 'All things are lawful for me'—but he will not be enslaved by anything (1 Corinthians 6:12). 'Food is meant for the stomach and the stomach for food'—but God will destroy both. The body is not meant for sexual immorality but for the Lord, and the Lord for the body (1 Corinthians 6:13). God raised the Lord and will also raise them by his power (1 Corinthians 6:14). Do they not know their bodies are members of Christ? Shall he take the members of Christ and make them members of a prostitute? Never! Do they not know that whoever is united to a prostitute becomes one body with her? As it is written: 'The two will become one flesh' (1 Corinthians 6:15–16). But the one who is united to the Lord becomes one spirit with him (1 Corinthians 6:17). Flee sexual immorality! Every other sin is outside the body, but the sexually immoral person sins against his own body (1 Corinthians 6:18). Or do they not know their body is a temple of the Holy Spirit within them, which they have from God, and that they are not their own? For they were bought with a price—so glorify God in your body (1 Corinthians 6:19–20).
12.historical_context: 'All things are lawful' (panta exestin) is almost certainly a Corinthian slogan being quoted and corrected by Paul; the repetition with a counter-argument ('but not all things are helpful'; 'but I will not be enslaved') is Paul's characteristic diatribe format when engaging opposing positions. The stomach-food analogy (v.13) may be another Corinthian argument: as food and stomach are made for each other and both will pass away, so sex and body are similarly transient and morally neutral. Paul's counter-argument is that the resurrection of the body makes bodily behavior permanently significant—bodies are not discarded after death but transformed. The temple imagery (v.19) applies the 1 Corinthians 3:16 corporate temple metaphor to the individual body, making the body the location of the Spirit's indwelling and therefore deserving of the same reverence as the temple of God. 'Bought with a price' (v.20) is the language of slave-redemption: the believer's body is no longer their own but belongs to the one who purchased them.
12.literary_structure: The passage takes the form of a progressive argument: Corinthian slogans quoted and corrected (v.12–13) → resurrection reframe of bodily significance (v.14) → union-with-Christ logic applied to sexual union (vv.15–17) → practical command (v.18) → temple and ownership grounds for the command (vv.19–20). The Genesis 2:24 citation ('two become one flesh,' v.16) is applied to the union with Christ (v.17) by contrast: sexual union creates a bodily one-flesh reality; spiritual union with Christ creates a spiritual one-spirit reality. The two unions are incompatible when the sexual union is with a prostitute because it creates a bodily union that contradicts the spiritual union with Christ.
12.theological_application: The theology of 1 Corinthians 6:12–20 is one of the most consequential NT texts for Christian sexual ethics. Paul's argument is not that sex is dirty but that the body is glorious—too glorious for casual use. The resurrection means bodies are not temporary containers to be used and discarded but the locus of God's indwelling Spirit and the future site of God's resurrection power. Sexual immorality is therefore not merely a failure of self-control but a violation of the body's deepest identity: it is the temple of the Holy Spirit, bought by Christ's blood, destined for resurrection. The 'flee' command (v.18) is one of the NT's most urgent imperatives—there is no negotiating with sexual temptation but only flight. The church must take this seriously in its formation of believers: sexual ethics are not peripheral restrictions imposed on reluctant bodies but the proper honoring of bodies that belong to the Lord and house his Spirit. 'Glorify God in your body' is the positive vision: the body as an instrument of worship, not merely a site of restraint.
12.christology: The christological density of this passage is remarkable: the body is for the Lord and the Lord is for the body (v.13); God raised the Lord Jesus and will raise our bodies by the same power (v.14); our bodies are members of Christ (v.15); union with Christ is a one-spirit union (v.17); we were bought with a price (v.20). The resurrection of Christ is the pivot: it permanently consecrates bodily existence as the theater of divine action, since the same God who raised Jesus will raise the very bodies that now house the Spirit. The 'Lord for the body' (v.13) is an astonishing claim—not only is the body made for the Lord, but the Lord is made for the body, committing himself permanently to its transformation and glory. Sexual immorality therefore involves taking what belongs to Christ (the body) and uniting it to what is incompatible with his purposes—a violation of the intimacy of the union between Christ and the believer that Paul elsewhere calls the marriage mystery of Christ and his church (Ephesians 5:31–32).
